::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Download Notebook
[Download as Jupyter Notebook](notebooks/acv_analysis.ipynb)
:::

## Learning Objectives

After completing this tutorial, you will be able to:

- Compute the variance of the ACV correction term from the two-sample-set structure
- Derive the optimal ACV weight $\eta^*$ and the variance reduction factor
  $\gamma = 1 - \frac{r-1}{r}\rho^2_{\alpha\kappa}$
- Derive the optimal ratio $r^*$ of LF to HF samples for a fixed computational budget
- Verify the theoretical variance reduction numerically


## Prerequisites

Complete [ACV Concept](acv_concept.qmd) and [Control Variate Analysis](control_variate_analysis.qmd)
before this tutorial.


## Setup and Notation

Let $f_\alpha$ and $f_\kappa$ be the high- and low-fidelity models with per-sample costs
$C_\alpha$ and $C_\kappa$, and population moments

$$
\mu_\alpha = \mathbb{E}_\theta[f_\alpha], \quad
\sigma^2_\alpha = \mathbb{V}_\theta[f_\alpha], \quad
\sigma^2_\kappa = \mathbb{V}_\theta[f_\kappa], \quad
C_{\alpha\kappa} = \mathrm{Cov}_\theta(f_\alpha, f_\kappa), \quad
\rho_{\alpha\kappa} = \frac{C_{\alpha\kappa}}{\sigma_\alpha \sigma_\kappa}.
$$

Draw $N$ shared samples $\mathcal{Z}_N$ and $rN$ LF-only samples
$\mathcal{Z}_{rN} \supset \mathcal{Z}_N$ ($r > 1$). Define

$$
\hat{\mu}_\alpha = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^N f_\alpha(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}), \quad
\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^N f_\kappa(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}), \quad
\hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN} = \frac{1}{rN}\sum_{k=1}^{rN} f_\kappa(\boldsymbol{\theta}^{(k)}).
$$

The ACV estimator is

$$
\hat{\mu}_\alpha^{\text{ACV}} = \hat{\mu}_\alpha + \eta\left(\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N - \hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN}\right).
$$ {#eq-acv}

::: {.callout-important}
## Sample structure matters
The inclusion $\mathcal{Z}_N \subset \mathcal{Z}_{rN}$ is not merely a convenience —
it is what makes the variance derivation below work. Because the $N$ shared samples appear
inside both $\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N$ and $\hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN}$, the correction term
$\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N - \hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN}$ has a specific covariance structure (derived
in @eq-correction-var) that would be different under other partitioning schemes. With two
models the nested structure is the only natural choice, but with three or more low-fidelity
models there are multiple valid ways to partition samples across models — leading to different
ACV variants (MFMC, GIS, GRD) with different variance formulas. See
[General ACV](acv_many_models_concept.qmd) for details.
:::


## Unbiasedness

$$
\mathbb{E}\!\left[\hat{\mu}_\alpha^{\text{ACV}}\right]
= \mu_\alpha + \eta\left(\mu_\kappa - \mu_\kappa\right) = \mu_\alpha. \qquad \square
$$

This holds for any $r > 1$ and any $\eta$ because both LF estimators are unbiased for $\mu_\kappa$.


## Variance of the Correction Term

Split $\hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN}$ into the shared and exclusive parts:

$$
\hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN}
= \frac{1}{rN}\sum_{k=1}^{rN} f_\kappa^{(k)}
= \frac{1}{r}\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N + \frac{1}{r}\cdot\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=N+1}^{rN} f_\kappa^{(k)}.
$$

Therefore

$$
\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N - \hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN}
= \frac{r-1}{r}\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N - \frac{1}{r}\cdot\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=N+1}^{rN} f_\kappa^{(k)}.
$$

The two terms on the right use **disjoint** sample sets, so they are independent. Taking
variance:

$$
\mathbb{V}\!\left[\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N - \hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN}\right]
= \frac{(r-1)^2}{r^2}\cdot\frac{\sigma^2_\kappa}{N}
+ \frac{1}{r^2}\cdot\frac{\sigma^2_\kappa}{N}
= \frac{r-1}{r}\cdot\frac{\sigma^2_\kappa}{N}.
$$ {#eq-correction-var}

Compared with the plain LF estimator variance $\sigma^2_\kappa / N$, the correction term
variance is scaled by $(r-1)/r < 1$. As $r \to \infty$ the correction becomes increasingly
accurate; as $r \to 1$ it vanishes to zero (but so does the correction itself).


## Covariance Between HF Estimator and Correction Term

The shared samples $\mathcal{Z}_N$ appear in both $\hat{\mu}_\alpha$ and $\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N$
but **not** in the exclusive LF samples. Therefore

$$
\mathrm{Cov}\!\left(\hat{\mu}_\alpha,\, \hat{\mu}_\kappa^N - \hat{\mu}_\kappa^{rN}\right)
= \mathrm{Cov}\!\left(\hat{\mu}_\alpha,\, \frac{r-1}{r}\hat{\mu}_\kappa^N\right)
= \frac{r-1}{r}\cdot\frac{C_{\alpha\kappa}}{N}.
$$ {#eq-correction-cov}


## Optimal Weight and Variance Reduction

The ACV estimator variance is (using the variance-of-sums formula):

$$
\mathbb{V}\!\left[\hat{\mu}_\alpha^{\text{ACV}}\right]
= \frac{\sigma^2_\alpha}{N}
+ \eta^2\frac{r-1}{r}\frac{\sigma^2_\kappa}{N}
+ 2\eta\frac{r-1}{r}\frac{C_{\alpha\kappa}}{N}.
$$

This is a quadratic in $\eta$. Setting the derivative to zero:

$$
\eta^* = -\frac{C_{\alpha\kappa}}{\sigma^2_\kappa}.
$$ {#eq-eta-star}

This is **identical** to the CVMC optimal weight — the extra LF samples do not change the
optimal correction direction, only the precision of the correction. Substituting $\eta^*$:

$$
\boxed{
\mathbb{V}\!\left[\hat{\mu}_\alpha^{\text{ACV}}\right]
= \frac{\sigma^2_\alpha}{N}\left(1 - \frac{r-1}{r}\,\rho^2_{\alpha\kappa}\right).
}
$$ {#eq-acv-var}

The variance reduction factor is $\gamma = 1 - \frac{r-1}{r}\rho^2_{\alpha\kappa}$. Two limits:

- $r \to 1^+$: $\gamma \to 1$ (no reduction — the correction is too noisy to help)
- $r \to \infty$: $\gamma \to 1 - \rho^2_{\alpha\kappa}$ (full CVMC reduction)


## Optimal Sample Allocation

For a fixed computational budget $P$, the cost constraint is

$$
N C_\alpha + r N C_\kappa = P
\implies N = \frac{P}{C_\alpha + r C_\kappa}.
$$

Substituting into @eq-acv-var, the estimator variance as a function of $r$ alone is

$$
\mathbb{V}\!\left[\hat{\mu}_\alpha^{\text{ACV}}\right]
= \frac{\sigma^2_\alpha (C_\alpha + r C_\kappa)}{P}
\left(1 - \frac{r-1}{r}\rho^2_{\alpha\kappa}\right).
$$ {#eq-acv-var-budget}

Minimising over $r > 1$, setting $\frac{d}{dr}\mathbb{V} = 0$ and solving:

$$
r^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_\alpha}{C_\kappa}\cdot\frac{\rho^2_{\alpha\kappa}}{1 - \rho^2_{\alpha\kappa}}}.
$$ {#eq-r-star}

This result has an intuitive interpretation. When $\rho_{\alpha\kappa}$ is large and $C_\kappa$
is small, the optimal $r^*$ is large — many cheap LF samples are worth buying to tighten
the correction. When $C_\kappa \approx C_\alpha$ or $\rho_{\alpha\kappa}$ is small, $r^*$
is close to 1 and investing in additional LF samples is not worthwhile.

::: {.callout-note}
## Connection to CVMC
When $r \to \infty$, the term $rNC_\kappa$ dominates the budget and $N \to 0$. The optimal
$r^*$ balances the benefit of a tighter correction against the cost of shrinking $N$.
CVMC is the idealised case where LF samples are free ($C_\kappa = 0$), giving $r^* = \infty$.
:::


## Numerical Verification

We verify @eq-acv-var and @eq-r-star using the tunable ensemble benchmark.

::: {#f147521d .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from pyapprox.typing.util.backends.numpy import NumpyBkd
from pyapprox.typing.benchmarks.instances.multifidelity.tunable_ensemble import (
    tunable_ensemble_3model,
)
from pyapprox.typing.statest.statistics import MultiOutputMean
from pyapprox.typing.statest.mc_estimator import MCEstimator
from pyapprox.typing.statest.acv import MFMCEstimator

bkd = NumpyBkd()
benchmark = tunable_ensemble_3model(bkd, theta1=np.pi / 2 * 0.95)
hf_model  = benchmark.models()[0]
lf_model  = benchmark.models()[1]
variable  = benchmark.prior()
nqoi      = hf_model.nqoi()

cov_exact     = benchmark.ensemble_covariance()[:2, :2]
cov_mat       = bkd.to_numpy(cov_exact)
sigma2_alpha  = cov_mat[0, 0]
sigma2_kappa  = cov_mat[1, 1]
C_alphakappa  = cov_mat[0, 1]
rho           = C_alphakappa / np.sqrt(sigma2_alpha * sigma2_kappa)

# Use exact benchmark covariance so empirical results match theory
stat = MultiOutputMean(nqoi, bkd)
stat.set_pilot_quantities(cov_exact)

stat_mc = MultiOutputMean(nqoi, bkd)
stat_mc.set_pilot_quantities(cov_exact[:1, :1])

print(f"rho = {rho:.4f}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
rho = 0.9720
```
:::
:::


::: {#cell-fig-acv-verification .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
N        = 50
n_trials = 2000
r_values = [1.5, 2, 3, 5, 10, 20, 50]

# MC variance baseline: sigma^2_alpha / N
mc_var = sigma2_alpha / N

from pyapprox.typing.statest.acv.allocation import ACVAllocationResult

cost_hf_unit = 1.0
cost_lf_unit = 0.001

empirical_reductions = []

for r in r_values:
    # Build ACV estimator with fixed N and rN
    est = MFMCEstimator(stat, costs=[cost_hf_unit, cost_lf_unit])
    target_cost = N * cost_hf_unit + r * N * cost_lf_unit

    # Manually set allocation with desired sample ratio r
    partition_ratios = bkd.asarray([float(r - 1)])
    npart = est._npartition_samples_from_partition_ratios(
        target_cost, partition_ratios
    )
    npart_int = bkd.asarray(
        [int(round(float(npart[k]))) for k in range(len(npart))]
    )
    nsamp = est._compute_nsamples_per_model(npart_int)
    actual_cost = float((nsamp * bkd.asarray([cost_hf_unit, cost_lf_unit])).sum())
    alloc = ACVAllocationResult(
        partition_ratios=partition_ratios,
        continuous_npartition_samples=npart,
        objective_value=bkd.asarray([0.0]),
        npartition_samples=npart_int,
        nsamples_per_model=nsamp,
        target_cost=target_cost,
        actual_cost=actual_cost,
        success=True,
    )
    est.set_allocation(alloc)

    acv_ests = np.empty(n_trials)
    for i in range(n_trials):
        np.random.seed(i)
        s_hf, s_lf = est.generate_samples_per_model(variable.rvs)
        acv_ests[i] = bkd.to_numpy(
            est([hf_model(s_hf), lf_model(s_lf)])
        )[0]
    empirical_reductions.append(np.var(acv_ests) / mc_var)

r_fine = np.linspace(1.01, 55, 300)
theory_line = 1 - (r_fine - 1) / r_fine * rho**2

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.5))
ax.plot(r_fine, theory_line, "--", color="#C0392B", lw=1.8,
        label=r"Theory: $1 - \frac{r-1}{r}\rho^2$")
ax.scatter(r_values, empirical_reductions, color="#2C7FB8", s=60, zorder=4,
           label=f"Empirical ($N={N}$, {n_trials} trials)")
ax.axhline(1 - rho**2, color="#27AE60", ls=":", lw=1.5,
           label=r"CVMC limit: $1 - \rho^2$")
ax.axhline(1.0, color="k", ls=":", lw=1, alpha=0.3, label="MC (no reduction)")
ax.set_xlabel(r"Sample ratio $r$", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel("Variance reduction factor $\\gamma$", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title("ACV variance reduction: theory vs empirical", fontsize=11)
ax.legend(fontsize=9)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![Empirical ACV variance reduction (dots) versus the theoretical prediction $1 - \frac{r-1}{r}\rho^2$ (dashed line) across a range of sample ratios $r$. The CVMC limit $1 - \rho^2$ is approached as $r$ grows. Each point uses $N = 50$ HF samples and 2000 independent trials.](acv_analysis_files/figure-html/fig-acv-verification-output-1.png){#fig-acv-verification width=661 height=420}
:::
:::


::: {#cell-fig-optimal-r .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
C_alpha = 10.0
C_kappa = 1.0
P       = 500.0

# Theoretical curve and optimum
r_fine2 = np.linspace(1.01, 30, 400)
N_of_r  = P / (C_alpha + r_fine2 * C_kappa)
var_of_r = sigma2_alpha / N_of_r * (1 - (r_fine2 - 1) / r_fine2 * rho**2)

r_star = np.sqrt(C_alpha / C_kappa * rho**2 / (1 - rho**2))
N_star = P / (C_alpha + r_star * C_kappa)
var_star = sigma2_alpha / N_star * (1 - (r_star - 1) / r_star * rho**2)

# Estimator allocation
est_opt = MFMCEstimator(stat, costs=[C_alpha, C_kappa])
est_opt.allocate_samples(P)
nspm = est_opt.nsamples_per_model()
n_hf_opt, n_lf_opt = int(nspm[0]), int(nspm[1])
r_est = n_lf_opt / n_hf_opt
var_est = float(est_opt.optimized_covariance()[0, 0])

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4.5))
ax.plot(r_fine2, var_of_r, color="#2C7FB8", lw=2,
        label="ACV variance at fixed budget $P$")
ax.axvline(r_star, color="#C0392B", ls="--", lw=1.8,
           label=rf"Theory $r^* = {r_star:.1f}$")
ax.plot(r_star, var_star, "o", color="#C0392B", ms=9, zorder=4)
ax.plot(r_est, var_est, "*", color="#27AE60", ms=14, zorder=5,
        label=rf"Estimator $r = {r_est:.1f}$")
ax.set_xlabel(r"Sample ratio $r$", fontsize=12)
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\mathbb{V}[\hat{\mu}_\alpha^{\mathrm{ACV}}]$", fontsize=12)
ax.set_title(
    rf"Optimal $r^*$ at fixed budget ($C_\alpha={C_alpha}$, $C_\kappa={C_kappa}$, $P={P}$)",
    fontsize=11,
)
ax.legend(fontsize=10)
ax.grid(True, alpha=0.2)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

print(f"Theoretical r* = {r_star:.2f}  (continuous N* = {N_star:.2f})")
print(f"Estimator   r  = {r_est:.2f}  (rounded   N  = {n_hf_opt}, rN = {n_lf_opt})")
print(f"Theoretical variance (continuous) = {var_star:.6f}")
print(f"Estimator   variance (rounded)    = {var_est:.6f}")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display}
![ACV estimator variance as a function of $r$ at fixed budget $P$ (solid blue), with the theoretical optimum $r^*$ marked (red dashed). The green star shows the `MFMCEstimator` allocation, which closely matches the theory.](acv_analysis_files/figure-html/fig-optimal-r-output-1.png){#fig-optimal-r width=660 height=419}
:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
Theoretical r* = 13.08  (continuous N* = 21.67)
Estimator   r  = 13.43  (rounded   N  = 21, rN = 282)
Theoretical variance (continuous) = 0.005884
Estimator   variance (rounded)    = 0.005980
```
:::
:::


::: {.callout-note}
## Integer rounding
The theoretical optimum uses continuous $N^*$ and $r^*$, but in practice we can only
evaluate a model an integer number of times. `allocate_samples` rounds $N$ and $rN$ to
integers, which shifts both the effective ratio and the variance. The green star in
@fig-optimal-r does not sit exactly on the red dot for this reason. The discrepancy
shrinks as the budget $P$ grows, because rounding a larger $N$ to an integer is a smaller
relative perturbation.
:::


## Key Takeaways

- The ACV correction term variance is $\frac{r-1}{r}\frac{\sigma^2_\kappa}{N}$, smaller
  than the plain LF estimator variance by the factor $(r-1)/r$
- The optimal weight $\eta^* = -C_{\alpha\kappa}/\sigma^2_\kappa$ is identical to CVMC
- The variance reduction factor $\gamma = 1 - \frac{r-1}{r}\rho^2_{\alpha\kappa}$
  interpolates between no reduction ($r \to 1$) and full CVMC reduction ($r \to \infty$)
- For a fixed budget, the optimal ratio is
  $r^* = \sqrt{\frac{C_\alpha}{C_\kappa}\frac{\rho^2}{1-\rho^2}}$


## Exercises

1. Verify algebraically that substituting $\eta^*$ from @eq-eta-star into the ACV variance
   expression yields @eq-acv-var.

2. Starting from @eq-acv-var-budget, derive $r^*$ from @eq-r-star by differentiating
   with respect to $r$ and setting the result to zero.

3. Show that as $C_\kappa \to 0$ (free LF evaluations), $r^* \to \infty$ and the ACV
   variance at the optimal allocation approaches the CVMC variance $\sigma^2_\alpha(1-\rho^2)/N$.

4. **(Numerical)** The verification above uses the exact benchmark covariance to set pilot
   quantities. Replace `stat.set_pilot_quantities(cov_exact)` with a pilot estimate from
   $N_{\text{pilot}}$ samples using `compute_pilot_quantities`. Try $N_{\text{pilot}} = 20$,
   $50$, and $200$. How does the pilot size affect the empirical variance reduction dots in
   @fig-acv-verification? Explain why small pilots cause the dots to deviate from the
   theoretical curve.

5. **(Challenge)** Suppose you have two low-fidelity models $f_{\kappa_1}$ and $f_{\kappa_2}$
   with different costs and correlations. Write down the two-control-variate ACV estimator
   and identify what additional terms appear in the variance compared to the single LF case.


## References

- [GGEJJCP2020] A. Gorodetsky, S. Geraci, M. Eldred, J. Jakeman. *A generalized approximate
  control variate framework for multifidelity uncertainty quantification.* Journal of
  Computational Physics, 408:109257, 2020.
  [DOI](https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcp.2020.109257)
